skills.teks_description: Investigate ecological relationships including predation, parasitism, commensalism, mutualism, competition
skills.prompt: This topic covers ecology and ecosystems for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS B.13(A)). Students should understand ecological relationships at multiple levels of organization. Key concepts: energy flow through trophic levels (producers, primary/secondary/tertiary consumers, decomposers), the 10% rule of energy transfer, food chains vs. food webs, biogeochemical cycles (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water, sulfur). Ecological relationships: predation (population regulation), parasitism (one benefits at other's expense), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), mutualism (both benefit), competition (intraspecific and interspecific affecting resource distribution). Ecosystem services: provisioning (food, water), regulating (climate, water purification), supporting (nutrient cycling, soil formation), cultural (recreation, aesthetics). Primary productivity: gross primary productivity (GPP) vs. net primary productivity (NPP = GPP - respiration); varies by biome. Keystone species and trophic cascades demonstrate how single species can regulate entire ecosystems. Vocabulary: trophic level, biomass, primary productivity, keystone species, indicator species, pioneer species, ecological succession, climax community, resilience, ecosystem services. Common misconceptions: ecosystems are static; top predators are most important.

skills.teks_description: Explain how environmental changes affect biodiversity and ecosystem stability
skills.prompt: This topic covers biodiversity for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS B.13(D)). Students should understand biodiversity levels, threats, and conservation strategies. Three levels of biodiversity: genetic diversity (variation within species), species diversity (number and relative abundance of species), ecosystem diversity (variety of habitats and ecological processes). Biodiversity hotspots: regions with exceptionally high species richness and endemism facing significant habitat loss. Threats to biodiversity (HIPPCO): Habitat loss (greatest threat), Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation. Island biogeography theory: larger islands closer to mainland support more species; applies to habitat fragments. Species-area relationship: S = cA^z. Key concepts: extinction rates (background vs. mass extinction), endangered vs. threatened species, endemic species, ecological redundancy, edge effects in habitat fragmentation. Conservation strategies: protected areas, habitat corridors, captive breeding, seed banks, legislation (Endangered Species Act, CITES). Vocabulary: biodiversity, endemic, invasive species, habitat fragmentation, edge effect, extinction, speciation, HIPPCO, conservation biology, flagship species, umbrella species. Common misconceptions: biodiversity only matters in rainforests; extinction is always caused by humans.

skills.teks_description: Analyze disruptions to matter cycling and energy flow through trophic levels
skills.prompt: This topic covers population ecology for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS B.13(B)). Students should analyze population dynamics and energy flow through ecological systems. Population growth models: exponential (J-curve, dN/dt = rN, unlimited resources) vs. logistic (S-curve, dN/dt = rN(K-N)/K, resources limit growth). Carrying capacity (K): maximum population size an environment can sustain. r-strategists vs. K-strategists: r-selected species (many offspring, little parental care, short lifespan) vs. K-selected species (few offspring, extensive parental care, long lifespan). Survivorship curves: Type I (low early mortality, high late), Type II (constant mortality), Type III (high early mortality). Density-dependent factors: competition, predation, disease, parasitism. Density-independent factors: natural disasters, climate events. Age structure diagrams: predict future growth (expanding, stable, declining populations). Demographic transition model: pre-industrial to post-industrial population patterns. Trophic levels: energy decreases at each level (ecological pyramids of energy, biomass, numbers). Vocabulary: birth rate, death rate, immigration, emigration, carrying capacity, r-strategist, K-strategist, age structure, demographic transition, total fertility rate, doubling time. Common misconceptions: human population can grow indefinitely; K is fixed.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate the role of water resources and impacts of pollution on aquatic systems
skills.prompt: This topic covers water resources and pollution for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(A)). Students should evaluate water resource management and pollution impacts on aquatic systems. Water distribution: 97.5% saltwater, 2.5% freshwater (most in ice caps and glaciers); only ~1% accessible for human use. Key concepts: groundwater (aquifers, water table, recharge zones), surface water (lakes, rivers, wetlands), watershed management. Water pollution types: point source (identifiable origin, e.g., factory discharge) vs. nonpoint source (diffuse, e.g., agricultural runoff). Major pollutants: nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus causing eutrophication and algal blooms), pathogens, heavy metals (mercury biomagnification), thermal pollution, sediment, synthetic chemicals (pesticides, pharmaceuticals), plastics. Eutrophication process: excess nutrients → algal bloom → decomposition → oxygen depletion → dead zone. Water treatment: primary (physical), secondary (biological), tertiary (chemical/advanced). Key issues: groundwater depletion (Ogallala Aquifer), ocean acidification, wetland destruction, dam impacts, water scarcity and conflict. Vocabulary: aquifer, watershed, eutrophication, dead zone, hypoxia, point source, nonpoint source, BOD, dissolved oxygen, desalination, water table, potable. Common misconceptions: water is a limitless resource; groundwater flows like underground rivers.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze sources, effects, and mitigation of air pollution
skills.prompt: This topic covers air pollution for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(B)). Students should analyze sources, effects, and mitigation strategies for air pollutants. Primary pollutants: emitted directly from sources (CO, SO2, NOx, particulate matter PM, VOCs, lead). Secondary pollutants: formed by chemical reactions in atmosphere (ozone O3, sulfuric acid, nitric acid, PAN, photochemical smog). Major issues: smog (industrial/London smog from SO2 and particulates; photochemical/LA smog from NOx and VOCs reacting in sunlight), acid deposition (SO2 and NOx combine with water to form sulfuric and nitric acid; pH < 5.6; damages aquatic ecosystems, forests, buildings), indoor air pollution (radon, CO, formaldehyde, asbestos, mold, tobacco smoke; often worse than outdoor in developing nations from cooking fires). Clean Air Act: regulates criteria pollutants (CO, SO2, NO2, O3, PM, Pb), established NAAQS. Mitigation strategies: catalytic converters, scrubbers, electrostatic precipitators, cap-and-trade programs, alternative energy, emission standards. Air Quality Index (AQI) measures pollution levels. Vocabulary: criteria pollutant, primary pollutant, secondary pollutant, smog, acid deposition, temperature inversion, VOC, particulate matter, catalytic converter, scrubber. Common misconceptions: all ozone is bad (stratospheric ozone is protective); clean-looking air is pollution-free.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate evidence and models related to global climate change
skills.prompt: This topic covers climate change for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(C)). Students should evaluate evidence and models related to global climate change. Greenhouse effect: natural process where atmospheric gases (CO2, CH4, N2O, H2O vapor, CFCs) trap infrared radiation, warming Earth's surface. Enhanced greenhouse effect: human activities increase greenhouse gas concentrations, amplifying warming. Evidence for climate change: rising global temperatures, melting ice sheets and glaciers, sea level rise, ocean acidification, shifting species ranges, increased extreme weather events, ice core data (CO2 and temperature correlation over 800,000 years). Carbon cycle disruption: burning fossil fuels, deforestation, cement production increase atmospheric CO2 from ~280 ppm (pre-industrial) to over 420 ppm. Climate models: computer simulations predicting future scenarios based on emission pathways (IPCC scenarios). Impacts: sea level rise threatening coastal areas, coral bleaching, changes in precipitation patterns, agricultural disruption, biodiversity loss, climate refugees. Mitigation: reduce emissions (renewable energy, efficiency, carbon tax), carbon capture and storage. Adaptation: sea walls, drought-resistant crops, infrastructure planning. Vocabulary: greenhouse gas, radiative forcing, albedo, carbon sink, feedback loop, IPCC, mitigation, adaptation, carbon footprint, ocean acidification. Common misconceptions: weather disproves climate change; the greenhouse effect is entirely human-caused.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze fossil fuel formation, extraction, and environmental impacts
skills.prompt: This topic covers fossil fuels and energy production for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.9(A)). Students should analyze fossil fuel formation, extraction, and environmental impacts. Fossil fuels: coal (formed from ancient plant material in swamps, most polluting), petroleum/oil (formed from marine organisms, used for transportation), natural gas (methane CH4, cleanest fossil fuel, often found with oil). Formation: millions of years of heat and pressure on organic matter. Extraction methods: surface mining (strip mining, mountaintop removal), subsurface mining, conventional drilling, hydraulic fracturing (fracking), offshore drilling, tar sands extraction. Environmental impacts: air pollution (CO2, SO2, NOx, particulates), water pollution (acid mine drainage, oil spills, fracking chemicals), habitat destruction, land subsidence, greenhouse gas emissions driving climate change. Energy concepts: net energy (EROI - energy returned on energy invested), energy efficiency, capacity factor. Coal-fired power plants: combustion heats water → steam → turbine → generator. Nuclear energy: fission of uranium-235, high energy density, radioactive waste concerns, meltdown risk, no direct CO2 emissions. Vocabulary: fossil fuel, nonrenewable, EROI, fracking, acid mine drainage, mountaintop removal, tar sands, carbon capture, energy density, capacity factor. Common misconceptions: fossil fuels will last forever; natural gas is completely clean.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Compare renewable and nonrenewable energy sources
skills.prompt: This topic covers renewable energy sources for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.9(B)). Students should compare renewable and nonrenewable energy sources, analyzing benefits and limitations. Solar energy: photovoltaic cells convert sunlight to electricity; passive solar design; concentrated solar power; intermittent, requires storage, declining costs. Wind energy: turbines convert kinetic energy; onshore and offshore; bird/bat mortality, visual impact, intermittent. Hydroelectric: dams convert flowing water's potential energy; reliable, but habitat disruption, sediment trapping, methane from reservoirs. Biomass/biofuels: ethanol from corn/sugarcane, biodiesel, wood; carbon neutral in theory but land use concerns. Geothermal: heat from Earth's interior; limited to tectonically active areas, reliable baseload power. Hydrogen fuel cells: H2 + O2 → H2O + electricity; clean but production often uses fossil fuels. Tidal and wave energy: emerging technologies. Key concepts: energy efficiency, grid integration, energy storage (batteries, pumped hydro), life-cycle analysis, distributed generation, net metering, feed-in tariffs, carbon neutrality, LCOE (levelized cost of energy). Vocabulary: renewable, photovoltaic, turbine, hydroelectric, biomass, geothermal, hydrogen fuel cell, intermittent, baseload, capacity factor, LCOE, net metering. Common misconceptions: renewable means zero environmental impact; solar panels don't work on cloudy days.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate waste management strategies including recycling and reduction
skills.prompt: This topic covers waste management for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(D)). Students should evaluate waste management strategies and their environmental implications. Waste hierarchy (most to least preferred): reduce, reuse, recycle, recover (energy), dispose. Municipal solid waste (MSW): household and commercial waste; composition varies by country (paper, food, plastics, metals, glass). Disposal methods: sanitary landfills (lined, leachate collection, methane capture; issues include groundwater contamination, space limitations), incineration (reduces volume by 90%, energy recovery possible; produces ash, air pollutants, requires scrubbers), open dumps (unregulated, common in developing nations). Recycling: closed-loop vs. open-loop, contamination issues, markets for recyclables, e-waste recycling challenges. Hazardous waste: toxic, flammable, corrosive, or reactive; RCRA regulates cradle-to-grave management; Superfund (CERCLA) cleans contaminated sites. Composting: aerobic decomposition of organic waste into soil amendment. Industrial ecology: waste from one process becomes input for another (circular economy). Vocabulary: MSW, landfill, leachate, incineration, recycling, composting, hazardous waste, e-waste, RCRA, Superfund, circular economy, life cycle analysis, source reduction. Common misconceptions: recycling solves the waste problem; biodegradable items decompose quickly in landfills.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze soil composition and agricultural practices
skills.prompt: This topic covers soil and agriculture for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(A)). Students should analyze soil composition and agricultural practices. Soil composition: minerals (45%), water (25%), air (25%), organic matter (5%); formed from weathering of parent rock and decomposition of organic material. Soil horizons: O (organic), A (topsoil, most fertile), B (subsoil, mineral accumulation), C (weathered parent material), R (bedrock). Soil properties: texture (sand, silt, clay proportions), structure, porosity, permeability, pH, cation exchange capacity. Agricultural practices: conventional (monoculture, synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, irrigation, mechanization) vs. sustainable (crop rotation, cover crops, integrated pest management, organic farming, no-till agriculture, agroforestry). Soil degradation: erosion (wind and water), compaction, salinization (from irrigation), desertification, loss of fertility, contamination. Green revolution: high-yield varieties, irrigation, fertilizers, pesticides; increased food production but environmental costs. Food security: population growth vs. food production, food distribution inequality. Vocabulary: humus, loam, topsoil, erosion, desertification, salinization, monoculture, polyculture, IPM, organic farming, GMO, green revolution, food security, agroforestry. Common misconceptions: soil is just dirt; organic farming cannot feed the world.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate land use practices and conservation strategies
skills.prompt: This topic covers land use and conservation for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(B)). Students should evaluate land use practices and conservation strategies. Land use categories: urban/suburban, agricultural, forestry, mining, recreation, wilderness. Urban sprawl: low-density development, car dependency, loss of farmland and habitat, increased impervious surfaces affecting water runoff. Smart growth: compact development, mixed-use zoning, public transit, green spaces. Forest management: clear-cutting (efficient but destructive), selective cutting (less impact), prescribed burns (reduce wildfire risk, ecological benefits), sustainable forestry certification (FSC). Public lands: national parks (preservation), national forests (multiple use), wildlife refuges, BLM lands, wilderness areas. Conservation approaches: preservation (keeping nature undisturbed) vs. conservation (sustainable use of resources). Protected areas: IUCN categories, marine protected areas, wildlife corridors connecting habitat fragments. Ecological restoration: returning degraded ecosystems to natural state; wetland restoration, stream restoration, prairie restoration. Land use planning tools: zoning, environmental impact assessments, conservation easements. Vocabulary: urban sprawl, smart growth, clear-cutting, selective cutting, multiple use, preservation, conservation, restoration, easement, impervious surface, greenway, buffer zone. Common misconceptions: national parks and national forests have the same purpose; development always destroys ecosystems.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze environmental legislation and regulatory frameworks
skills.prompt: This topic covers environmental laws and regulations for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.11(A)). Students should analyze major environmental legislation and regulatory frameworks. Key U.S. environmental laws: NEPA (1970, requires Environmental Impact Statements for federal projects), Clean Air Act (1970, regulates air emissions, criteria pollutants, NAAQS), Clean Water Act (1972, regulates point source discharges, NPDES permits, wetland protection), Endangered Species Act (1973, protects threatened and endangered species and critical habitats), RCRA (1976, regulates hazardous waste cradle-to-grave), CERCLA/Superfund (1980, cleans up contaminated sites), TSCA (regulates chemical substances), Safe Drinking Water Act (sets maximum contaminant levels). Regulatory agencies: EPA (federal enforcement), state environmental agencies. International agreements: Montreal Protocol (successfully phased out CFCs), Kyoto Protocol (greenhouse gas reduction targets), Paris Agreement (limit warming to 1.5-2°C). Key concepts: command-and-control regulation vs. market-based approaches (cap-and-trade, carbon tax, green subsidies), cost-benefit analysis, precautionary principle, environmental justice. Vocabulary: NEPA, EIS, NAAQS, NPDES, EPA, cap-and-trade, precautionary principle, environmental justice, regulation, compliance, enforcement, mitigation. Common misconceptions: environmental laws prevent all pollution; international agreements are legally binding and enforceable.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate the effects of toxins and pollutants on ecosystems and human health
skills.prompt: This topic covers environmental toxicology for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(E)). Students should evaluate the effects of toxins and pollutants on ecosystems and human health. Key concepts: dose-response relationship (the dose makes the poison), LD50 (lethal dose for 50% of test population), threshold dose, acute vs. chronic exposure, synergistic effects (combined effect greater than sum of individual effects). Bioaccumulation: buildup of toxins in an individual organism over time. Biomagnification: increasing concentration of toxins through trophic levels (e.g., DDT in birds of prey, mercury in fish). Persistent organic pollutants (POPs): resist degradation, bioaccumulate (DDT, PCBs, dioxins). Endocrine disruptors: mimic or block hormones (BPA, phthalates, atrazine); affect reproduction and development at very low doses. Heavy metals: lead (neurological damage), mercury (Minamata disease), arsenic, cadmium. Risk assessment: hazard identification, dose-response assessment, exposure assessment, risk characterization. Routes of exposure: ingestion, inhalation, dermal contact. Vulnerable populations: children, elderly, pregnant women, low-income communities. Vocabulary: toxin, toxicant, dose-response, LD50, bioaccumulation, biomagnification, persistent organic pollutant, endocrine disruptor, carcinogen, mutagen, teratogen, risk assessment. Common misconceptions: natural substances are safe; any amount of a chemical is dangerous.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze human population dynamics and demographic trends
skills.prompt: This topic covers human population growth for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.7(A)). Students should analyze human population dynamics, demographic trends, and their environmental implications. Current global population: approximately 8 billion; growth rate declining but population still increasing. Demographic transition model: Stage 1 (high birth and death rates, stable population), Stage 2 (death rate falls, rapid growth), Stage 3 (birth rate falls, growth slows), Stage 4 (low birth and death rates, stable), Stage 5 (birth rate below death rate, declining). Key indicators: crude birth rate (CBR), crude death rate (CDR), total fertility rate (TFR, replacement level ≈ 2.1), infant mortality rate, life expectancy, doubling time (Rule of 70: doubling time = 70/growth rate%). Age structure diagrams: expanding (triangular, growing populations in developing nations), stable (columnar), contracting (inverted, aging populations in developed nations). Factors affecting growth: access to education (especially women's education), contraception availability, economic development, cultural and religious values, government policies (China's one-child policy, pronatalist policies). Environmental impacts: resource consumption, ecological footprint, IPAT model (Impact = Population × Affluence × Technology). Vocabulary: demographic transition, TFR, CBR, CDR, infant mortality, doubling time, age structure, ecological footprint, IPAT, urbanization, migration. Common misconceptions: overpopulation is solely a developing-world problem; simply reducing population solves environmental issues.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate sustainability practices and their environmental impacts
skills.prompt: This topic covers sustainability for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.11(B)). Students should evaluate sustainability practices and their environmental impacts. Sustainability definition: meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet their needs (Brundtland Commission, 1987). Three pillars: environmental (ecological integrity), economic (viable economy), social (social equity). Key concepts: ecological footprint (amount of land/water needed to support a person's lifestyle), carrying capacity, renewable vs. nonrenewable resources, natural capital (ecosystem services, biodiversity, resources). Sustainable practices: sustainable agriculture (organic farming, IPM, crop rotation, permaculture), sustainable energy (renewables, energy efficiency), sustainable forestry (FSC certification), green building (LEED certification, energy-efficient design), sustainable transportation (public transit, EV, bike infrastructure). Circular economy: design out waste, keep products and materials in use, regenerate natural systems; contrast with linear economy (take-make-dispose). Indicators: UN Sustainable Development Goals (17 SDGs), Human Development Index, Genuine Progress Indicator vs. GDP. Vocabulary: sustainability, ecological footprint, natural capital, circular economy, LEED, cradle-to-cradle, life cycle assessment, triple bottom line, SDGs, carrying capacity, renewable, nonrenewable. Common misconceptions: sustainability means going without; economic growth and sustainability are incompatible.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate evidence related to global warming and greenhouse effect
skills.prompt: This topic covers global warming and the greenhouse effect for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(C)). Students should evaluate evidence related to global warming mechanisms and impacts. Greenhouse effect mechanism: solar radiation passes through atmosphere → Earth's surface absorbs and re-emits as infrared radiation → greenhouse gases absorb and re-emit IR radiation → warming of lower atmosphere and surface. Major greenhouse gases ranked by contribution: water vapor (most abundant, natural feedback), CO2 (fossil fuels, deforestation; ~420 ppm and rising), methane CH4 (25x more potent than CO2 per molecule; sources: livestock, rice paddies, landfills, natural gas), nitrous oxide N2O (agriculture, fossil fuels), CFCs (synthetic, also destroy ozone). Global warming potential (GWP): compares warming effect of different gases relative to CO2 over 100 years. Evidence: instrumental temperature records show ~1.1°C warming since pre-industrial times, ice core records show CO2-temperature correlation, satellite data, ocean heat content, retreating glaciers. Positive feedback loops: ice-albedo feedback (melting ice reduces reflectivity → more warming), permafrost thaw (releases CH4), water vapor feedback. Negative feedbacks: increased plant growth (absorbs CO2), cloud formation. Vocabulary: greenhouse effect, greenhouse gas, GWP, radiative forcing, albedo, feedback loop, ice core, carbon dioxide equivalent, forcing agent, climate sensitivity. Common misconceptions: the greenhouse effect is unnatural; CO2 is the only greenhouse gas.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze atmospheric changes including ozone depletion
skills.prompt: This topic covers ozone depletion for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.10(B)). Students should understand stratospheric ozone chemistry and distinguish ozone depletion from global warming. Ozone layer: concentrated in stratosphere (15-35 km altitude); absorbs harmful UV-B and UV-C radiation, protecting life on Earth. Ozone formation: O2 + UV → 2O, then O + O2 → O3 (Chapman cycle). Ozone destruction by CFCs: UV breaks C-Cl bond in CFCs releasing chlorine radical; Cl + O3 → ClO + O2, then ClO + O → Cl + O2; one chlorine atom can destroy ~100,000 ozone molecules (catalytic cycle). Ozone hole: severe thinning over Antarctica during spring (September-November) due to polar stratospheric clouds facilitating CFC-ozone reactions. Effects of ozone depletion: increased UV-B reaching surface causing skin cancer, cataracts, immune suppression, damage to phytoplankton, crop damage. Montreal Protocol (1987): international agreement phasing out CFCs and other ozone-depleting substances; considered most successful environmental treaty; ozone layer recovering slowly. Alternatives: HCFCs (transitional, less destructive), HFCs (no ozone impact but potent greenhouse gases). Vocabulary: stratosphere, ozone layer, CFC, UV radiation, catalytic cycle, ozone hole, Montreal Protocol, polar stratospheric cloud, chlorine radical, HCFC, HFC. Common misconceptions: the ozone hole causes global warming; the ozone hole is a permanent gap; spray cans still contain CFCs.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze impacts of deforestation on ecosystems and climate
skills.prompt: This topic covers deforestation for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(C)). Students should analyze the impacts of deforestation on ecosystems, climate, and biodiversity. Causes of deforestation: agricultural expansion (cattle ranching, soy and palm oil plantations, slash-and-burn farming), logging (legal and illegal), mining, urbanization, infrastructure development, fuelwood collection. Scale: approximately 10 million hectares lost annually, primarily in tropical regions (Amazon, Congo Basin, Southeast Asia). Environmental impacts: biodiversity loss (tropical forests contain over 50% of terrestrial species), climate change (forests store ~25% of global carbon; deforestation releases CO2 and reduces carbon sequestration capacity), disruption of water cycle (reduced transpiration leads to decreased rainfall), soil erosion and degradation (tree roots stabilize soil; removal leads to erosion, landslides, sedimentation), flooding (reduced water absorption), habitat fragmentation. Social impacts: displacement of indigenous communities, loss of traditional knowledge, short-term economic gains vs. long-term ecological costs. Solutions: reforestation and afforestation, sustainable forestry practices (selective logging, certification), agroforestry, protected areas, REDD+ (Reducing Emissions from Deforestation and forest Degradation), consumer awareness (sustainable palm oil, FSC-certified wood), payments for ecosystem services. Vocabulary: deforestation, reforestation, afforestation, carbon sequestration, slash-and-burn, selective logging, REDD+, agroforestry, habitat fragmentation, old-growth forest. Common misconceptions: planting trees fully offsets deforestation; all logging is bad.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Evaluate environmental impacts of urban development
skills.prompt: This topic covers urbanization for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.8(D)). Students should evaluate the environmental impacts of urban development and strategies for sustainable cities. Urbanization trends: over 55% of world population lives in cities (projected 68% by 2050); fastest growth in developing nations; megacities (10+ million people). Environmental impacts: heat island effect (cities 1-3°C warmer due to concrete, asphalt, reduced vegetation, waste heat), increased impervious surfaces (increased stormwater runoff, flooding, water pollution), air pollution (vehicle emissions, industrial sources, smog), water pollution (runoff carrying oil, heavy metals, nutrients), habitat destruction and fragmentation, increased resource consumption and waste generation, noise and light pollution. Urban sprawl: low-density, automobile-dependent development consuming farmland and natural habitats; increases driving, energy use, and infrastructure costs. Smart growth strategies: mixed-use development, compact building design, walkable neighborhoods, public transit investment, urban growth boundaries, green spaces and parks, infill development, transit-oriented development. Green infrastructure: green roofs, rain gardens, permeable pavement, urban forests, bioswales reduce runoff and cool cities. Vocabulary: urbanization, urban sprawl, smart growth, heat island, impervious surface, infill, green infrastructure, transit-oriented development, megacity, brownfield, greenfield. Common misconceptions: cities are always worse for the environment than suburbs; urbanization cannot be sustainable.

skills.teks_description: AP Environmental Science: Analyze ethical perspectives on environmental issues
skills.prompt: This topic covers environmental ethics for AP Environmental Science students (TEKS ESS.11(C)). Students should analyze ethical perspectives on environmental issues and their influence on policy. Major ethical frameworks: anthropocentrism (human-centered, nature valued for human benefit; supports resource management for sustained yield), biocentrism (all living things have inherent value; opposes actions harming individual organisms), ecocentrism (ecosystems and ecological processes have intrinsic value; Aldo Leopold's land ethic: 'A thing is right when it tends to preserve the integrity, stability, and beauty of the biotic community'). Key environmental philosophies: preservation ethic (keep nature undisturbed, John Muir), conservation ethic (wise use of resources, Gifford Pinchot), deep ecology (radical restructuring of human-nature relationship), ecofeminism (links environmental degradation to patriarchal systems), environmental justice (fair treatment regardless of race, income, or nationality). Tragedy of the commons (Garrett Hardin): shared resources are overexploited when individuals act in self-interest; solutions include regulation, privatization, and community management. Intergenerational equity: responsibility to future generations. Key debates: economic development vs. environmental protection, rights of nature, global vs. local responsibility. Vocabulary: anthropocentrism, biocentrism, ecocentrism, land ethic, preservation, conservation, environmental justice, tragedy of the commons, intrinsic value, instrumental value, stewardship, precautionary principle. Common misconceptions: environmentalism is anti-human; ethics and science are separate.
